The specific physical router in an HSRP group that is currently responsible for forwarding all traffic sent to the virtual gateway address. Only one router can be active at a time.
A router that monitors the active router's status and is prepared to take over the forwarding role immediately if the active device stops responding. This ensures continuous network connectivity.
An optional feature that allows a higher-priority router to regain the 'Active' role as soon as it comes back online, even if another router is currently servicing the virtual gateway.
A configurable value (default 100) used to determine which router becomes the active gateway in an HSRP group. The device with the highest priority is elected as the primary forwarder.
An ARP message sent by an FHRP router when it takes over the active role. It informs all switches on the segment to update their MAC tables so traffic is redirected to the new active device.
A one-to-one mapping between an internal private IP address and a specific external public IP address. It is typically used for internal servers that must be reachable from the internet.
A translation process where internal private addresses are mapped to the first available public address from a pre-defined pool. This allows for efficient use of a limited number of public IPs.
Also known as NAT Overloading, it allows multiple private IP addresses to be mapped to a single public IP address using unique source port numbers to distinguish between sessions.
The actual private IP address assigned to a host on the internal network. It is the address the device uses to communicate within its own local segment.
The public IP address that represents an internal host to the outside world. It is the translated address seen by devices on the internet.
The public IP address assigned to a host on an external network, such as a web server. It is the destination address as seen from the internal network.
The IP address of an external host as it appears to the internal network. In most standard NAT configurations, this is identical to the outside global address.
A defined range of public IP addresses that a router can use for dynamic translation. The router assigns an address from this pool to internal devices as they request internet access.
The base Cisco IOS command used to define which internal traffic should be translated. It is used for both static mappings and dynamic translations using ACLs and pools.
The specific addition to a NAT command that enables Port Address Translation. It allows a single public IP address, such as the one on the router's WAN port, to support hundreds of internal devices.
A protocol used to synchronize the clocks of computer systems over a network. Accurate time is essential for log file analysis, security certificates, and scheduled network tasks.
A hierarchical level that indicates the distance of a device from an authoritative time source. Stratum 0 is a high-precision clock, while Stratum 1 is a server directly connected to it.
A configuration where a network device requests and receives time updates from a designated NTP server. This ensures the device's clock remains accurate and synchronized.
A Cisco IOS command that configures a router to act as an authoritative NTP server for other devices on the network, even if it is not synchronized to an external source.
A security feature that ensures a device only synchronizes its time with trusted and verified NTP servers. It protects the network from malicious time spoofing attacks.
A network protocol that automatically assigns IP addresses and other configuration parameters like subnet masks and default gateways to devices on a network.
The four-step sequence a client uses to obtain an IP address: Discover (broadcast), Offer (from server), Request (from client), and Acknowledgment (from server).
A router feature that forwards DHCP broadcast requests from clients to a server on a different subnet. This allows for a single centralized DHCP server to support multiple logical networks.
The specific Cisco IOS command used to enable a DHCP relay agent on an interface. it converts local broadcasts into unicast packets directed at the central server's IP address.
A range of IP addresses within a DHCP pool that the server is instructed not to assign to clients. These are typically reserved for static assignment to devices like routers and servers.
The specific amount of time for which a client is permitted to use an assigned IP address. The client must renew the lease periodically to maintain its network connectivity.
A configuration on a DHCP server that ensures a specific device (identified by its MAC address) always receives the same IP address every time it connects to the network.
An essential network service that translates human-readable hostnames (like www.google.com) into their corresponding IP addresses so that devices can locate one another.
A request where a client asks a DNS server to resolve a name, and the server takes responsibility for finding the answer by querying other servers if necessary.
A request where a DNS server provides the best answer it currently has, which might be a referral to another server that is more likely to know the correct IP address.
The most common type of DNS record, which maps a hostname to a 32-bit IPv4 address. It is the fundamental link between human-readable names and network locations.
A DNS record type used to map a hostname to a 128-bit IPv6 address. It performs the same function as a standard 'A' record but for the next-generation IP protocol.
A DNS record that acts as an alias, pointing one hostname to another canonical hostname. This allows multiple names to resolve to the same underlying IP address.
A temporary storage area on a host or server that keeps track of recently resolved DNS queries. This significantly speeds up subsequent requests for the same hostname.
A DNS record that specifies which server is authoritative for a given domain. It tells other servers where to find the official records for that specific part of the DNS hierarchy.
The centralized software system used to monitor and manage network devices. It sends requests to and receives information from SNMP agents running on routers, switches, and servers.

A software component running on a network device that maintains its management data and responds to queries from the SNMP manager. it can also proactively send notifications when specific events occur.
A structured hierarchical database used by SNMP that defines all the objects a device can monitor. Each object is identified by a unique Object Identifier (OID).
A message sent by an SNMP manager to an agent to retrieve the value of a specific management object, such as the current traffic level on a switch port.
A command sent by an SNMP manager to an agent to modify a configuration value on a device, such as shutting down a physical interface or changing a system name.
An unsolicited notification sent by an SNMP agent to a manager to report a significant event, such as a link failure or an authentication error. it is an unreliable (UDP) message.
A notification similar to a trap, but it requires an acknowledgement from the SNMP manager. if no ACK is received, the agent will retransmit the message, making it more reliable.
The most commonly used version of SNMP. it introduces bulk transfers and better error handling but still relies on simple 'community strings' for security, which are sent in plain text.
The most secure version of SNMP, providing robust authentication and encryption. It uses USM (User-based Security Model) and VACM (View-based Access Control Model) to protect management data.
A simple password used in SNMP v1 and v2c to authenticate communication between the manager and the agent. There are typically two types: 'read-only' and 'read-write'.
A standard protocol for sending system log or event messages to a central server. it allows administrators to monitor device health, security events, and configuration changes from a single location.
A standardized scale from 0 to 7 used to rank the importance of a log message. Level 0 is the most critical (Emergency), while Level 7 is the least critical (Debugging).
The highest syslog severity level, indicating that the entire system is unusable. This is reserved for catastrophic failures that require immediate attention.
A syslog severity level indicating that a specific component or process has failed. While serious, it typically does not cause a total system outage.
A syslog severity level that alerts administrators to potential problems that might become serious if not addressed. it is used for non-critical errors or unusual events.
A syslog severity level used for significant normal operational events, such as an interface changing state (UP/DOWN) or a configuration change being made.
The lowest syslog severity level, containing highly detailed information typically used only by engineers for troubleshooting specific software issues.
A Cisco IOS command that configures a device to store log messages in its local RAM. This allows administrators to view the recent history of events even if no external syslog server is available.
A configuration command that defines the minimum severity level of messages that should be sent to an external syslog server. Only messages at this level or higher will be forwarded.
A label used to categorize the source of a log message, such as 'auth', 'cron', or 'local0'. it allows the syslog server to sort and store messages from different systems into separate files.
A feature that allows a router to forward DHCP broadcast requests from clients to a server on a different network. it is essential for centralizing IP address management across multiple subnets.
The Cisco IOS command used to enable a DHCP relay agent. it converts incoming local broadcasts for several protocols, including DHCP and DNS, into unicast packets for a specific server.
A configuration on a Cisco router interface (using 'ip address dhcp') that allows the device to automatically obtain its own IP address and subnet mask from a central server.
The defined range of IP addresses that a DHCP server is permitted to assign to clients on a specific network segment. Each pool is associated with a single subnet.
A specialized DHCP configuration used in VoIP environments to provide the IP addresses of TFTP servers to IP phones, allowing them to download their configuration and firmware.
A vendor-specific DHCP option often used to provide the IP addresses of Wireless LAN Controllers to lightweight access points during the join process.
A process where a DHCP server pings an IP address before assigning it to a client to ensure no other device is already using it statically. This prevents IP address conflicts on the network.
A Layer 2 security feature that monitors DHCP traffic on a switch. it blocks unauthorized DHCP servers and prevents various attacks like DHCP starvation and spoofing.
A database maintained by a DHCP server that maps the MAC address of each client to its assigned IP address and the remaining duration of its lease.
The pre-configured duration for which a DHCP client is allowed to use its assigned IP address. it is typically set to 24 hours on many Cisco systems but is highly configurable.
A set of technologies used to manage network traffic and ensure that critical applications receive the necessary bandwidth, low latency, and minimal packet loss required for proper operation.

The process of identifying and grouping network traffic into different categories based on specific criteria like port numbers, IP addresses, or application types. it is the first step in applying QoS policies.

The action of changing specific bits in a packet header (like DSCP or CoS) to identify its priority level. This allows other network devices to recognize and apply the appropriate QoS treatment to the traffic.
A 6-bit field in the IPv4 or IPv6 header used for advanced traffic marking. it provides up to 64 different priority levels, allowing for granular control over network performance.
A 3-bit marking field found in the 802.1Q Ethernet header. it is used to prioritize traffic at Layer 2 across trunk links, providing 8 different priority levels.
A mechanism that stores packets in separate memory buffers when an outgoing interface is congested. The router then decides which queue to service first based on the assigned priority levels.

A set of techniques, such as Weighted Random Early Detection (WRED), that proactively drop packets when buffers begin to fill up. This prevents total congestion and ensures that high-priority traffic is protected.

A traffic management tool that strictly enforces a maximum bit rate by immediately dropping any packets that exceed the configured limit. it is typically used at the network edge to prevent resource abuse.
A technique that limits the output bit rate of an interface by temporarily buffering excess packets in a queue. This smooths out traffic bursts and provides a more consistent data flow than policing.
The specific point in a network where QoS markings on incoming packets are either accepted and trusted or stripped and reassigned. it is typically located at the access layer switch port.
A queuing method that provides a strict priority queue for time-sensitive traffic like voice. This ensures that these packets are always sent first, minimizing delay and jitter.
The variation in the delay of received packets. High jitter can cause significant quality issues for real-time applications like VoIP and video conferencing, making QoS essential.
The total capacity of a network link to transmit data. QoS tools ensure that this resource is allocated fairly and that critical applications always have the minimum amount they need to function.
A cryptographic network protocol used for secure remote access to a device's command-line interface. Unlike Telnet, it encrypts all communication, including usernames and passwords.
The modern and much more secure version of the SSH protocol. it uses stronger encryption algorithms and is the standard requirement for managing Cisco networking hardware.
The logical ports on a Cisco device used for remote management access. For security, these should be configured to only allow SSH connections using the 'transport input ssh' command.
A set of cryptographic keys (public and private) generated on a router or switch to enable SSH. The private key remains on the device, while the public key is used for secure session negotiation.
A required configuration command for enabling SSH on a Cisco device. it provides the necessary suffix for generating the device's unique RSA encryption keys.
A security configuration where a remote user's credentials are checked against a username and password stored directly in the device's own running configuration.
A legacy remote access protocol that sends all data in plain text. it is highly insecure and should be disabled on all production network devices in favor of SSH.
A very simple, connectionless (UDP) protocol used for transferring files between network devices. it is commonly used for backing up configurations and downloading IOS images in local environments.
A standard connection-oriented (TCP) protocol used for moving files between a client and a server. it is more reliable and supports larger file sizes than TFTP but requires more overhead.
The specific FTP transfer mode used for moving non-text files like Cisco IOS images. it ensures that the file content is transmitted bit-for-bit without any character translation.
The non-volatile storage on a Cisco device where the IOS operating system image is typically stored. Administrators use TFTP or FTP to upload new images to this memory for upgrades.
The configuration file stored in NVRAM that is loaded by a device when it boots up. it is a best practice to regularly back this file up to a remote TFTP or FTP server.
The standard Cisco IOS command used to upload the device's current active configuration to a remote TFTP server for backup and disaster recovery purposes.
A secure alternative to FTP and TFTP that uses the SSH protocol to transfer files between devices. it provides both the encryption of SSH and the file management capabilities of RCP.
An FTP connection method where the client initiates both the control and data connections to the server. This is often necessary when the client is located behind a NAT device or firewall.
A specialized feature in Cisco IOS that can automatically back up a device's configuration to a remote server whenever a change is made. it provides a history of configurations for auditing.
A command used to check the integrity of a file (like an IOS image) after it has been transferred. it typically uses an MD5 hash to ensure the file was not corrupted during the transfer.
A weakness in a system, process, or design that could be exploited by a malicious actor to compromise security. Common examples include software bugs or poorly configured hardware.
Any potential danger that could take advantage of a vulnerability to cause harm. it represents the possibility of a malicious act like data theft, service disruption, or unauthorized access.
A specific tool, technique, or piece of code used to take advantage of a vulnerability. it is the actual mechanism through which a threat actor gains access or causes damage.
The actions taken to reduce the risk or impact of a threat. This includes applying software patches, configuring firewalls, and implementing strong authentication policies.
An attack method that relies on human interaction to trick people into revealing sensitive information or breaking security procedures. Examples include phishing and impersonation.
A common social engineering attack where fraudulent emails or messages are sent to trick users into providing credentials or clicking on malicious links that install malware.
Malicious software designed to infiltrate, damage, or gain unauthorized access to a computer system. This broad category includes viruses, worms, trojans, and ransomware.
A trial-and-error method used to guess passwords or encryption keys. Attackers use automated tools to try thousands of combinations until the correct one is found.
Security measures designed to prevent unauthorized people from physically touching network equipment. Examples include locked server rooms, security cameras, and biometric scanners.
An educational program designed to teach employees about security risks and best practices. it is a critical defense against social engineering and human error.
The Cisco IOS command used to set a highly secure, hashed password for accessing privileged EXEC mode. it is much more secure than the older 'enable password' command.
A local security setting that requires a password for anyone physically connecting a terminal to the device's console port. it is the first line of defense for physical access.
A password requirement for remote management access via Telnet or SSH. Configuring this is mandatory for allowing any remote CLI interaction with a Cisco device.
A global Cisco IOS command that provides basic encryption for all plain-text passwords in the configuration file. While not robust, it prevents casual viewing of sensitive data.
A line configuration command that instructs the device to check a user's credentials against its own local database of usernames and passwords rather than a simple line password.
A security requirement that forces users to provide two or more different types of evidence to verify their identity, such as a password and a one-time code from a smartphone.
A policy that requires passwords to be long and include a mix of uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and special symbols to resist guessing and brute-force attacks.
An electronic file used to prove the identity of a device or user over a network. it is a highly secure alternative to passwords and is the basis for many encryption protocols.
Authentication methods based on unique physical characteristics, such as fingerprints, facial recognition, or iris scans. They provide high security as they are difficult to replicate.
A security policy that requires users to change their passwords periodically. This limits the amount of time an attacker can use a compromised set of credentials.
A fundamental security concept where users and devices are granted only the minimum level of access necessary to perform their jobs. This limits the potential damage from a security breach.
A framework for managing network security that stands for Authentication (who you are), Authorization (what you can do), and Accounting (what you actually did).
A malicious attack that attempts to make a network resource or service unavailable to its intended users, typically by overwhelming it with a flood of useless traffic.
A type of malware that encrypts a victim's files and demands payment in exchange for the decryption key. it is one of the most significant threats to modern organizations.
An attack that targets a software vulnerability that is unknown to the vendor or the public. Because no patch exists yet, these threats are extremely difficult to defend against.
An attack where a malicious actor secretly intercepts and possibly alters the communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly with each other.
A Cisco IOS command used to display a legal warning message to anyone attempting to log into the device. it is an important part of a corporate security policy.
A configuration that automatically disconnects an inactive remote session after a certain period. This prevents unauthorized users from taking over an abandoned management connection.
A centralized server used to manage AAA services for a large number of network devices. it allows for a single point of management for user accounts and security policies.
A physical device, like a key fob or smart card, that generates a changing code used for multi-factor authentication. it provides a high level of security for sensitive access.

A permanent encrypted tunnel between two locations, such as a head office and a branch. it allows devices at both sites to communicate securely over the internet as if they were on the same private network.
A temporary secure connection initiated by an individual user to gain access to a corporate network. it typically uses software on the user's device and can be based on SSL/TLS or IPsec protocols.
A suite of protocols used to secure IP communications by authenticating and encrypting each IP packet in a data stream. it is the industry standard for creating secure site-to-site VPN tunnels.
An IPsec protocol that provides data confidentiality (encryption) and authentication. it encapsulates the original packet, ensuring that its contents cannot be read or tampered with during transit.
A protocol used in IPsec to establish a secure session between two devices. it handles the negotiation of encryption algorithms and the secure exchange of cryptographic keys.
A sequential list of permit or deny statements used by a router or switch to filter network traffic. it can be based on various criteria like IP addresses, protocols, and port numbers.
A type of ACL that only filters traffic based on the source IP address. it uses numbers from 1–99 or 1300–1999 and should be applied as close to the destination as possible.
A more granular type of ACL that filters traffic based on source and destination IP addresses, protocols, and port numbers. it uses numbers 100–199 or 2000–2699 and is applied close to the source.

An invisible statement at the end of every ACL that automatically drops any traffic that did not match an earlier permit rule. To allow any traffic, there must be at least one explicit permit statement.
An ACL identified by a descriptive text string instead of a number. This makes configuration more organized and allows for easier editing of individual lines within the list.
A 32-bit string used in ACL rules to specify which parts of an IP address to examine. A '0' bit means 'match exactly,' while a '1' bit means 'ignore'.
A Layer 2 security feature that acts as a firewall between untrusted hosts and trusted DHCP servers. it prevents malicious actors from setting up a rogue DHCP server on the network.
A table maintained by a switch with DHCP snooping enabled. it tracks the MAC address, IP address, lease time, and port for every client, serving as a reference for other security features.
A security feature that validates ARP packets in a network. it uses the DHCP snooping binding table to ensure that only legitimate ARP requests and responses are forwarded, preventing ARP spoofing.
A feature on a Cisco switch that restricts the number of MAC addresses allowed on an interface. it can be configured to automatically shut down a port if an unauthorized device is connected.
A port security configuration that allows a switch to dynamically learn a MAC address and save it permanently to its running configuration. This avoids manual entry of every authorized device.
The three possible actions a switch can take when a violation occurs: Shutdown (default, port goes to err-disabled), Restrict (log event, drop traffic), and Protect (drop traffic silently).
The first component of the AAA framework, which verifies the identity of a user or device. This is typically done through a combination of usernames and passwords or digital certificates.
The second component of AAA, which determines the specific permissions and resources a verified user is allowed to access. it defines what commands a user can run or what data they can view.
The final component of AAA, which tracks and records the actions performed by a user during their session. This creates an audit trail for billing, security analysis, and troubleshooting.
A switch port, typically connected to a legitimate DHCP server or another switch, that is allowed to send all types of DHCP messages. All other ports are considered untrusted by default.
An access port where end-user devices are connected. The switch blocks any DHCP server responses (like Offers or ACKs) from these ports to prevent rogue server attacks.
A malicious technique where an attacker sends forged ARP messages onto a local network to associate their MAC address with the IP of a legitimate server, allowing them to intercept traffic.
A specific type of ARP packet that threat actors can use to proactively update the ARP tables of other devices on a network with false information. DAI is used to mitigate this threat.
Unique identifiers assigned to each rule in a named or modern numbered ACL. They allow for the surgical insertion or deletion of specific rules without rewriting the entire list.
A configuration keyword used to add descriptive comments to an ACL. These comments are visible in the configuration and help explain the purpose of the filtering rules to other engineers.
A specialized network device, such as a Cisco ASA firewall or a router, that serves as the endpoint for VPN tunnels. it handles all encryption and decryption tasks for the remote connections.
A VPN configuration that allows a remote user to access the internet directly while simultaneously maintaining a secure tunnel to the corporate network for private resources.
A type of remote access VPN that uses the standard encryption built into web browsers. it is easy to deploy as it does not require specialized client software for basic access.
A standard networking protocol that provides centralized AAA management. it combines authentication and authorization into a single process and uses UDP for transmission.
The original security protocol for 802.11 wireless networks. it is now considered extremely insecure because its encryption keys can be easily cracked in minutes using freely available software.
An intermediate security standard designed to fix the weaknesses of WEP. it introduced the TKIP encryption protocol and provided a much-needed security boost before the finalization of WPA2.
The long-standing standard for wireless security that introduced hardware-based AES encryption. it provides robust protection for both personal and enterprise networks and is still widely used today.
The latest evolution of wireless security that addresses several vulnerabilities in WPA2. it introduces Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE) to protect against offline dictionary attacks.
The key exchange protocol used in WPA3 Personal. it replaces the PSK exchange of WPA2, providing much stronger protection for users who choose weak or easy-to-guess passwords.
An encryption protocol used in the original WPA standard. it was designed to run on older WEP hardware via software updates, but it has since been deprecated in favor of the more secure AES.
The specific encryption mechanism used by WPA2 and WPA3. it provides data confidentiality, integrity, and origin authentication using the highly secure Advanced Encryption Standard algorithm.
A common authentication method where all users on a wireless network use the same static password. it is the most frequent choice for home and small office Wi-Fi installations.

A high-security authentication framework that requires each user to provide unique credentials (like a username and password) to a RADIUS server before they are granted access to the wireless network.
The underlying protocol used by 802.1X to carry authentication data between the client, access point, and RADIUS server. it supports various methods like PEAP, EAP-TLS, and EAP-FAST.
The section in the Cisco Wireless LAN Controller GUI used to define the Layer 2 and Layer 3 security parameters for a WLAN. it is where you select WPA2 and configure the pre-shared key.
A sub-tab in the WLAN configuration GUI where you choose the encryption standard (like WPA2) and the authentication key management method (like PSK or 802.1X).
A setting in the WLC GUI that specifies how encryption keys are generated and distributed. For a standard home-style setup, this is where you would enable the PSK option.
The two formats for entering a pre-shared key in the WLC GUI. ASCII allows for human-readable passwords (8-63 characters), while Hex requires a 64-digit hexadecimal string.
A critical checkbox in the WLC GUI that must be selected for a wireless network to become active. Even if perfectly configured, the WLAN will not broadcast until it is explicitly enabled.
The advanced encryption mode used in WPA3 to provide even stronger data protection than the CCMP mode used in WPA2. it is required for the highest levels of wireless security.
A security property provided by WPA3 that ensures even if a password is later compromised, an attacker cannot decrypt wireless traffic that was captured in the past.
A security feature that encrypts the management traffic between an AP and a client. it is optional in WPA2 but mandatory in WPA3 to prevent deauthentication attacks.
A configuration screen in the WLC GUI where you define the IP address and shared secret of an external AAA server. This is necessary for implementing 802.1X Enterprise security.
An option in the WLC GUI that determines if the AP should include the SSID in its beacon frames. Disabling this makes the network 'hidden' to casual scans.
The use of software to automatically configure, manage, and monitor network devices. it reduces manual errors, improves consistency, and allows for faster deployment of network services.

A model where each network device is managed individually using its own control plane. Configuration is typically done manually via the CLI, making it slow and prone to human error in large environments.
A management model where a centralized software controller manages multiple network devices. it provides a global view of the network and allows for automated, policy-based configuration.
The logical part of a network device that makes decisions about where traffic should be sent. In SDN, this is often moved to a centralized controller to simplify management.
The hardware component of a network device that performs the actual forwarding of packets. it follows the instructions provided by the control plane to move data at high speeds.
A communication protocol (like OpenFlow or NETCONF) used by a controller to manage and configure network devices. it allows the controller to push policies down to the data plane.
An interface (typically REST-based) that allows applications and management software to communicate with a network controller. it enables high-level automation and orchestration of network services.
The physical infrastructure, including cabling and hardware devices, that provides the foundational connectivity for a network. it is responsible for the actual delivery of packets.
A logical network created on top of a physical underlay. it uses encapsulation (like VXLAN) to create virtual connections that are independent of the physical topology.
The combination of an underlay and an overlay network working together as a single integrated system. it provides a scalable, automated, and highly flexible networking environment.
A powerful management and automation platform for enterprise networks. it provides a centralized dashboard for design, provisioning, policy management, and troubleshooting using SDN principles.
A networking philosophy where administrators define high-level business goals (intent), and the network automation software automatically translates those goals into device-specific configurations.
An architectural style for creating web services that use standard HTTP methods to interact with resources. it is the most common type of API used for network automation.
The standard actions used in RESTful communication: GET (retrieve), POST (create), PUT (update), and DELETE (remove). They correspond to the basic CRUD operations.
The four basic functions of persistent storage: Create, Read, Update, and Delete. These are mapped to HTTP methods in RESTful network management interfaces.
An open-source automation tool that uses SSH to manage network devices. it is 'agentless,' meaning no software needs to be installed on the devices themselves, and uses YAML for configuration.
A configuration management tool that typically uses an agent-based model. it relies on a domain-specific language to define the desired state of network devices and ensures they stay in compliance.
An automation platform that uses an agent-based architecture and Ruby-based 'recipes' to manage configurations. it is highly flexible but requires more programming knowledge than tools like Ansible.
A lightweight data-interchange format that is easy for humans to read and write and easy for machines to parse. it is the standard format for data encoding in most modern network APIs.
A collection of key-value pairs wrapped in curly braces { }. Each key is a string followed by a colon and the associated value, making the data highly structured and searchable.
An ordered list of values wrapped in square brackets [ ]. Values within the array can be strings, numbers, objects, or even other arrays, allowing for complex data structures.
A YAML file in Ansible that contains a set of 'plays' or instructions to be executed on a group of network devices. it serves as the master blueprint for an automation task.
A key property of many automation tools where running the same task multiple times has no additional effect after the first successful execution. it ensures the network stays in the desired state.
The specific address used to identify a resource in a REST API, such as a particular switch port or a VLAN configuration. it is the target of an HTTP request.
The actual data sent or received in an API request or response, typically formatted in JSON or XML. it contains the specific configuration details or status information being exchanged.
A technique where a packet is encapsulated inside another protocol header to be transported across the underlay network. VXLAN is the most common example used in modern data center fabrics.
A feature that uses AI and machine learning to monitor network performance and provide proactive troubleshooting insights. it helps administrators identify and fix issues before they impact users.
A human-friendly data serialization language commonly used for configuration files and automation scripts. its clean, indentation-based structure makes it the preferred choice for Ansible playbooks.
The process of verifying the identity of a script or application before it is allowed to access a network controller. Common methods include API keys, basic auth, or OAuth tokens.
One of the original protocols used in software-defined networking to allow a controller to directly manipulate the forwarding tables of a switch. it is a foundational concept in the history of SDN.
